nodes.label: Explicitly NOT recommended (would change the research object)
nodes.norm_label: explicitly not recommended (would change the research object)

nodes.label: Memora (hybrid sub-quadratic architecture)
nodes.rationale: Combines LocalAttention (sliding-window + GQA + RoPE + QK-Norm) with GatedLinearAttention (GLA) recurrent layers for unbounded context at ~126M params. Critical invariant: the chunked training path (_chunked) and recurrent reference (_recurrent) must stay numerically equivalent, asserted in models/memora.py __main__; GLA stability comes from never materializing absolute e^{-Gc}, only bounded differences Gc_i - Gc_j <= 0. GLA layers deliberately skip RoPE since position lives in the recurrent state.

nodes.label: Cortex / Sparse-Cortex (ternary + GDN + SWA hybrid architecture)
nodes.rationale: Brain-inspired architecture combining BitNet b1.58 ternary weights, a hybrid GDN+SWA token mixer (~3 GDN layers per 1 SWA layer), ReLU^2 FFN sparsity, and (phase 3+) Mixture-of-Recursions adaptive depth. Embeddings, RMSNorm, and LM head are deliberately kept bf16 and never ternarized. Built as a staged plan — one lever at a time, each measured against the GPT-2 baseline before adding the next.
nodes.norm_label: cortex / sparse-cortex (ternary + gdn + swa hybrid architecture)

nodes.label: BitLinear (native ternary weight linear layer)
nodes.rationale: Drop-in replacement for nn.Linear: weights quantized to {-1,0,+1} via absmean (gamma=mean(|W|)), activations quantized to int8 via absmax per token, matmul becomes pure add/subtract. Backward uses a Straight-Through Estimator over a full-precision latent 'ghost' weight. Golden rules: never ternarize embeddings/RMSNorm/LM head (keep bf16); activations must be int8 never ternary (else the model collapses); train natively ternary from initialization, never post-training quantization (PTQ); no biases in BitLinear layers or norms; use ~2x the bf16 learning rate with a two-phase weight-decay schedule.

nodes.label: Gated DeltaNet (GDN)
nodes.rationale: Linear attention with delta-rule updates and gating; fixed-size recurrent state gives bounded VRAM and linear cost in sequence length. Gating enables fast erasure, the delta rule enables targeted updates. Its fixed-size state cannot store everything, which is exactly why it is paired with Sliding Window Attention layers — do not remove SWA thinking it's redundant, you would lose local recall.

nodes.label: Sliding Window Attention (SWA)
nodes.rationale: Exact softmax attention restricted to a local window (e.g. 512 tokens). Recovers the precise local recall that GDN's compressed fixed-size state loses. Removing SWA layers loses recall — treated as a hard rule (checklist item, cortex.md sec 13).

nodes.label: Squared ReLU (ReLU^2) activation
nodes.rationale: f(z) = max(0,z)^2. Induces ~90-95% emergent activation sparsity per-token (the 'lazy neuron phenomenon') without a predictor or extra machinery. At inference only the W_down columns for non-zero neurons need be computed. Optional L1 regularization on hidden activations can be added during training to push sparsity further. Alternative: dReLU / TurboSparse-style double ReLU.

nodes.label: Mixture-of-Recursions (MoR)
nodes.rationale: Phase-3+ adaptive-depth mechanism: a single weight-tied block is re-looped up to R times per token (e.g. R=3), with a router assigning recursion depth per token by learned difficulty (not post-hoc chain-of-thought). Double benefit: weight-tying reduces parameter count/VRAM, routing reduces FLOPs by only re-looping hard tokens. Requires a mandatory router homeostasis auxiliary loss (sigmoid depth probabilities + regularization) to prevent depth collapse (router always picking min or max depth). Supports per-recursion KV caching for still-active tokens only.

nodes.label: Mixture-of-Depths (MoD)
nodes.rationale: Simpler precursor to MoR for phase 2 if MoR proves too complex up front: a per-block router lets only top-k tokens through the block, the rest skip via the residual. Fixed capacity gives a static compute graph, easier to implement than MoR.

nodes.label: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)
nodes.rationale: Late-phase, scale-only lever: replaces the FFN with N experts, top-k activated per token (e.g. N=8, k=2). Does NOT reduce VRAM — all experts must reside in memory to be routable — and underperforms at small scale; its compute gain only pays off once the model is large (~40B target). Requires mandatory load-balancing auxiliary loss (coeff ~0.01) or a few experts monopolize traffic while others atrophy.

nodes.label: Gated Linear Attention (GLA)
nodes.rationale: Linear attention with a fixed-size recurrent state used by Memora's recurrent_layers for unbounded context; chunked-training and recurrent-inference paths must remain numerically equivalent (regression-guarded).

nodes.label: Straight-Through Estimator (STE)
nodes.rationale: Backward pass treats the forward quantization (BitLinear's ternary rounding) as identity: dL/dW ~= dL/dW_q, so gradients flow to a continuous full-precision latent weight even though the forward transmission is discrete.

nodes.label: GDN-H1 interleaving pattern (3 GDN : 1 SWA)
nodes.rationale: Recommended layer arrangement: interleave roughly 3 GDN layers per 1 SWA layer (9 GDN + 3 SWA across 12 layers in the Phase-1 124M config). GDN compresses long history, SWA guarantees exact local recall — considered the best current compromise for small models.

nodes.label: VRAM vs Compute independence principle
nodes.rationale: Central framing of the design: VRAM (storage) and compute (FLOPs/energy/speed) are independent axes — a lever that crushes one does not necessarily touch the other. Per cortex.md sec 1's table: ternary weights (BitNet b1.58) are the primary VRAM lever (mult->add) and also help compute; hybrid GDN+SWA attention bounds VRAM (no exploding KV-cache) and cuts compute (linear vs quadratic); ReLU^2 activation sparsity only helps compute; MoR (weight-tying) helps both VRAM and compute; MoE only helps compute, not VRAM, and is reserved for scale. Winning duo for VRAM specifically: ternary + weight-tying via MoR.

nodes.label: Equal-budget comparison protocol
nodes.rationale: Core evaluation methodology: novel sub-quadratic architectures are pitted against a reference GPT-2 Small under an equal-budget protocol — identical wall-clock seconds or identical step count per model — then compared on validation loss/perplexity, keeping the comparison fair across architectures of different shapes.

nodes.label: Memora reaches lower train loss than GPT-2 Small at equal tokens-seen
nodes.rationale: Across the full ~150M tokens_seen range plotted, the Memora curve (yellow) tracks visibly below the GPT-2 Small curve (blue) in train/loss: the gap opens early (~10-20M tokens, Memora ~4.7 vs GPT-2 ~5.5) and persists, with GPT-2 catching up somewhat by ~140M tokens but Memora still finishing lower (~3.6 vs ~3.8). Supports the equal-budget comparison protocol described in CLAUDE.md where sub-quadratic architectures are pitted against GPT-2 Small on validation loss/perplexity under matched token/step budget.
